title: Proper proximality in non-positive curvature
overall_summary: The paper proves proper proximality for several classes of countable groups acting on nonpositively curved spaces: groups with proper nonelementary rank-one CAT(0) actions, nonelementary CAT(0) cubical groups, groups acting on locally finite thick affine buildings, and many hierarchically hyperbolic groups including mapping class groups. The core method is dynamical: construct compact boundary G-spaces carrying diffuse or quasi-invariant measures and prove that every sequence escaping compact sets has a subsequence with north-south or almost-everywhere convergence on the boundary; this verifies Boutonnet-Ioana-Peterson proper proximality. For CAT(0) cube complexes, the paper combines this boundary criterion with Caprace-Sageev rank rigidity to reduce to rank-one factors. For the original graph-product von Neumann algebra problem, the most useful content is the collection of abstract proximality criteria, the product/finite-family mechanisms, and the CAT(0) cube-complex proof template, since irreducible graph products often have non-product, rank-one-like boundary dynamics.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 1.1 (Boutonnet-Ioana-Peterson proper proximality for countable groups)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group. An ultrafilter ω on G is nonprincipal if every finite subset of G has ω-measure 0. If K is a compact G-space and η is a diffuse probability measure on K, meaning η has no atoms, then a nonprincipal ultrafilter ω on G is η-proximal if for every h in G one has lim_{g→ω}((gh)·η - g·η) = 0 in the weak-star topology on signed measures on K. The group G is properly proximal if there exist finitely many compact G-spaces K_1,...,K_l and diffuse probability measures η_i on K_i such that: first, no K_i carries a G-invariant probability measure; second, for every nonprincipal ultrafilter ω on G, there exists i such that ω is η_i-proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition recalled from BIP21. The paper emphasizes that it is often easier to prove this definition through sequential dynamical criteria on compact boundaries, developed in the subsequent lemmas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.3 (properly proximal proper action implies properly proximal group)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group and let X be a proper metric space equipped with a proper isometric G-action. Assume the G-action on X is properly proximal in the following sequential sense: there are finitely many compact metrizable G-spaces K_1,...,K_l, none carrying a G-invariant probability measure, and diffuse probability measures η_i on K_i, such that for every sequence (g_n) in G escaping every compact subset of X, there exist an index i and a subsequence (g_{σ(n)}) such that for every h in G, lim_{n→∞}(g_{σ(n)}hη_i - g_{σ(n)}η_i) = 0 weak-star. Then G is properly proximal in the ultrafilter sense of Definition 1.1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a nonprincipal ultrafilter ω on G. For every i and h, compactness of the probability-measure space on K_i gives a weak-star ultralimit η_{i,h} = lim_{g→ω} ghη_i. Given a finite subset F of G containing the identity and small neighborhoods V_{i,h} of η_{i,h}, the set of g such that ghη_i lies in V_{i,h} for all i and h in F belongs to ω, hence is infinite. Since the measure spaces are metrizable, one chooses a sequence of pairwise distinct elements g_n such that g_nhη_i converges to η_{i,h} simultaneously for all i and h in F. Properness of the G-action on X implies that a subsequence of (g_n) escapes every compact subset of X. By sequential proper proximality of the action, after passing to a further subsequence there is an i such that g_{σ(n)}hη_i - g_{σ(n)}η_i tends to 0 for every h in F. Therefore η_{i,h} = η_{i,id} for all h in F. Since F was arbitrary and there are only finitely many i, a finite-intersection or pigeonhole argument over an exhaustion of G by finite sets gives one i for which η_{i,h} = η_{i,id} for all h in G. Thus ω is η_i-proximal. As the same K_i have no invariant probability measures and η_i are diffuse, the BIP definition is satisfied.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.4 (products of properly proximal actions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k be a positive integer. For each i in {1,...,k}, let G_i be a countable group acting by isometries on a proper metric space X_i, and assume the G_i-action on X_i is properly proximal in the sequential action sense. Let G be a subgroup of G_1 × ... × G_k whose projection to each factor G_i is surjective. Then the induced G-action on X_1 × ... × X_k is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use, for the G-action, the finite union of the compact spaces that witness proper proximality of each factor action, with G acting through the corresponding coordinate projection. Surjectivity of G → G_i ensures that if one of these compact G_i-spaces had a G-invariant probability measure, then it would have a G_i-invariant probability measure, which is excluded. If a sequence g_n = (g_{n,1},...,g_{n,k}) in G escapes every compact subset of the product X_1 × ... × X_k, then after passing to a subsequence at least one coordinate sequence g_{n,i} escapes every compact subset of X_i. Proper proximality of the G_i-action gives a compact space K and diffuse measure η in the i-th family, plus a further subsequence such that g_{n,i}aη - g_{n,i}η tends to 0 for every a in G_i. For h in G, take a to be the i-th coordinate of h; this gives g_nhη - g_nη → 0 for the G-action.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.5 (measure accumulation under pointwise accumulation control)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group acting on a compact metrizable space K. Let (g_n) be a sequence in G, let λ be a probability measure on K, and let A be a Borel subset of K. Suppose that for λ-almost every x in K, every accumulation point of the sequence (g_nx) belongs to A. Then every weak-star accumulation point ν of the sequence of measures (g_nλ) satisfies ν(A) = 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites FLM18, Lemma 8.3. The standard argument uses compact metrizability and regularity. If L is a compact subset of K \ A, then for almost every x the compact cluster set of (g_nx) is disjoint from L, so for that x there is some positive neighborhood radius, depending on x, such that eventually g_nx avoids that neighborhood of L. By decomposing the full-measure set according to a countable list of radii and using dominated convergence, one obtains arbitrarily small upper bounds on g_nλ of some open neighborhood of L along the sequence. Portmanteau then gives ν(L) = 0. Inner regularity of ν on K \ A gives ν(K \ A) = 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.6 (north-south-type dynamical criterion with one compact space)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group and X a proper metric space with an isometric G-action. Assume there is a compact metrizable G-space K with no G-invariant probability measure, and a G-invariant Borel subset K* of K containing a Borel subset homeomorphic to a Cantor set. Assume further that for every sequence (g_n) in G escaping every compact subset of X, there are a subsequence (g_{σ(n)}) and points ξ_- and ξ_+ in K such that for every ξ in K* \ {ξ_-}, the sequence g_{σ(n)}ξ converges to ξ_+. Then the G-action on X is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since K* contains a Borel Cantor subset, choose a diffuse probability measure η on K with η(K*) = 1. Given an escaping sequence, pass to the subsequence and points ξ_-, ξ_+ provided by the hypothesis. For every h in G, the measure hη is still diffuse and supported on K*, because K* is G-invariant; hence hη gives zero mass to the exceptional singleton {ξ_-}. For hη-almost every ξ, g_{σ(n)}ξ → ξ_+. Applying Lemma 1.5 with A = {ξ_+} yields g_{σ(n)}hη → δ_{ξ_+}. The same holds for h = id, so g_{σ(n)}hη - g_{σ(n)}η → 0 for every h. This is exactly sequential proper proximality of the action.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.8 (almost-everywhere convergence criterion with finitely many compact spaces)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group acting by isometries on a proper metric space X. Let K_1,...,K_l be compact metrizable G-spaces, none carrying a G-invariant probability measure. For each i let η_i be a quasi-invariant probability measure on K_i. Assume that for every sequence (g_n) in G escaping every compact subset of X, there exist an index i, a subsequence (g_{σ(n)}), and a point ξ_+ in K_i such that for η_i-almost every ξ in K_i, g_{σ(n)}ξ converges to ξ_+. Then the G-action on X is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix an escaping sequence and choose i, a subsequence, and ξ_+ as in the hypothesis. For every h in G, quasi-invariance of η_i implies that hη_i has the same null sets as η_i, so g_{σ(n)}ξ → ξ_+ for hη_i-almost every ξ. Lemma 1.5 gives g_{σ(n)}hη_i → δ_{ξ_+}. Taking h = id gives g_{σ(n)}η_i → δ_{ξ_+}, hence g_{σ(n)}hη_i - g_{σ(n)}η_i → 0 for every h. This verifies the sequential definition of a properly proximal action.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3 (free rank-one subgroup from a nonelementary rank-one CAT(0) action)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a proper CAT(0) space and let G act nonelementarily by isometries on X, where nonelementary means that G fixes no point of X and has no finite orbit in the visual boundary ∂∞X. Assume G contains a rank-one isometry, meaning an isometry with an invariant axis that bounds no half-plane. Then G contains a nonabelian free subgroup whose action on X is proper, nonelementary, and contains a rank-one element.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Hamenstädt, G contains two rank-one elements g and h with disjoint fixed point sets {g^{-∞},g^{+∞}} and {h^{-∞},h^{+∞}} in ∂∞X. Choose pairwise disjoint neighborhoods U_g^+, U_g^-, U_h^+, U_h^- in the visual compactification X ∪ ∂∞X, with a point x outside their union. Rank-one elements act with north-south dynamics, so for sufficiently large n the powers g^{±n} and h^{±n} send the complement of the corresponding repelling neighborhood into the attracting neighborhood. The ping-pong lemma gives a nonabelian free subgroup H generated by large powers of g and h. The same ping-pong setup shows that nontrivial elements move x into the chosen neighborhoods and that the H-orbit of x is free and discrete. If the H-action were not proper, infinitely many distinct h_n would move some y a uniformly bounded distance; by the triangle inequality they would move x a uniformly bounded distance, contradicting discreteness in the proper space X. The subgroup is nonelementary and contains rank-one powers of g and h.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5 (Cantor set of uniformly Morse rays in the CAT(0) visual boundary)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a proper CAT(0) space admitting a nonelementary isometric action of a group G with a rank-one element, and fix a basepoint x_0 in X. Then there exist a Morse gauge N and a topological embedding of a Cantor set into the visual boundary ∂∞X such that for every image point ξ, the geodesic ray [x_0,ξ) is N-Morse. Here N-Morse means that every (K,C)-quasigeodesic segment with endpoints on the ray stays in the N(K,C)-neighborhood of the ray.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 2.3, pass to a subgroup H acting properly and nonelementarily with a rank-one element. Yang's theorem supplies constants K,C,L, an infinitely ended rooted simplicial tree T, and a quasi-isometric embedding f:T→X with f(root)=x_0 such that the image of every infinite ray based at the root is an L-strongly contracting (K,C)-quasigeodesic ray. Sultan's result implies these images are uniformly Morse for some gauge N'. Cordes's Morse-boundary functoriality then gives a Morse gauge N such that the geodesic ray in X with the same endpoint as f(r) is N-Morse for every ray r in T, and the induced map on Morse boundaries ∂_M T → ∂_M X is a topological embedding into the subspace of N-Morse rays. Since T is a tree, its Morse boundary equals its visual boundary, a Cantor set. On a fixed Morse-gauge stratum, the topology agrees with the induced visual-boundary topology in the CAT(0) space, giving an embedded Cantor subset of ∂∞X.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.6 (no invariant probability on the visual boundary in the rank-one nonelementary case)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a proper CAT(0) space and let G act by isometries on X. Assume the G-action is nonelementary and G contains a rank-one element. Then the visual boundary ∂∞X carries no G-invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let g be a rank-one element. Rank-one isometries act on X ∪ ∂∞X with north-south dynamics, so for any probability measure ν on ∂∞X, every weak-star accumulation point of g^nν is supported on the two fixed points {g^{-∞},g^{+∞}}. Thus any G-invariant probability measure must be supported on this two-point set. Hamenstädt's theorem gives another rank-one element with disjoint fixed point set. Applying the same argument to it forces the invariant measure to be supported on a disjoint two-point set, impossible for a probability measure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.7 (visibility-boundary Borel set containing a Cantor set)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a proper CAT(0) space and let G be a countable group acting nonelementarily by isometries on X with a rank-one element. Let (∂∞X)_vis be the subset of the visual boundary consisting of visibility points, namely points ξ such that every η in ∂∞X can be joined to ξ by a geodesic in X. Then (∂∞X)_vis contains a G-invariant Borel subset K* which contains a Borel subset homeomorphic to a Cantor set.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Charney-Sultan prove that every Morse geodesic ray in a proper CAT(0) space has a visibility endpoint. Proposition 2.5 gives a Cantor set of endpoints of uniformly Morse rays, hence a Cantor subset of (∂∞X)_vis. Taking the union of its G-translates gives a G-invariant subset K*. Since G is countable and the original Cantor set is Borel, this union is Borel, and it remains inside the visibility boundary because visibility is G-invariant.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.8 (Papasoglu-Swenson dynamics on visibility points)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a proper CAT(0) space and let G act by isometries on X. If (g_n) is a sequence in G escaping every compact subset of X, then there exist a subsequence (g_{σ(n)}) and points ξ_- and ξ_+ in ∂∞X such that for every visibility point ξ in (∂∞X)_vis \ {ξ_-}, the sequence g_{σ(n)}ξ converges to ξ_+ in ∂∞X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If ξ is a visibility point and ξ' is any point of ∂∞X, then ξ and ξ' are joined by a geodesic. This geodesic cannot bound a half-plane, otherwise the boundary circle of that half-plane would contain points not joinable to ξ. By Bridson-Haefliger, this implies the Tits distance d_T(ξ,ξ') is greater than π. Hence a Tits ball of radius π contains at most one visibility point. Papasoglu-Swenson's theorem for sequences escaping compact subsets of a proper CAT(0) space gives, after passing to a subsequence, attracting and repelling boundary points with convergence outside a Tits ball of radius π. Since that ball contains at most one visibility point, convergence holds for every visibility point except possibly one.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1 (proper proximality of rank-one CAT(0) actions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group and X a proper CAT(0) metric space with an isometric G-action. Assume the G-action is nonelementary, meaning G fixes no point of X and has no finite orbit in ∂∞X, and assume G contains a rank-one isometry. Then the G-action on X is properly proximal. In particular, if the G-action on X is proper, then G is a properly proximal group.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the abstract criterion Lemma 1.6 with K = ∂∞X. Lemma 2.6 shows K has no G-invariant probability measure. Lemma 2.7 provides a G-invariant Borel subset K* of the visibility boundary containing a Cantor set, so K supports a diffuse probability measure concentrated on K*. Lemma 2.8 supplies the required one-exception north-south convergence for every sequence escaping compact subsets of X. Lemma 1.6 therefore gives proper proximality of the action. If the action is proper, Lemma 1.3 converts proper proximality of the action into proper proximality of the countable group G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2 (product version for rank-one CAT(0) actions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k be a positive integer. For each i in {1,...,k}, let X_i be a proper CAT(0) space and let G_i act by isometries on X_i. Let G be a countable subgroup of G_1 × ... × G_k which acts properly on X_1 × ... × X_k. Assume that for every i, the projection π_i(G) acts nonelementarily on X_i and contains a rank-one element. Then G is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each i, apply Theorem 2.1 to the countable group π_i(G) acting on X_i; this gives a properly proximal action of π_i(G) on X_i. Regard G as a subgroup of the product of its images π_i(G), so its projections are surjective. Lemma 1.4 gives proper proximality of the G-action on the product X_1 × ... × X_k. Since the G-action on this product is assumed proper, Lemma 1.3 implies that G is properly proximal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.9 (proper proximality of nonelementary CAT(0) cubical groups)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group acting properly and nonelementarily by cubical automorphisms on a proper finite-dimensional CAT(0) cube complex X. Then there exist a G-invariant subcomplex X' of X and a finite-index subgroup G_0 of G such that the G_0-action on X' is properly proximal. Consequently G is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Caprace-Sageev first gives a G-invariant subcomplex X' on which the action is essential and remains nonelementary. Their product decomposition theorem gives, after passing to a finite-index subgroup G_0, a product decomposition X' = X'_1 × ... × X'_p into irreducible CAT(0) cube complexes preserved by G_0. The G_0-action on each factor remains nonelementary. Caprace-Sageev rank rigidity then says each irreducible factor action contains a rank-one isometry. By Theorem 2.1, each factor action is properly proximal. Lemma 1.4 implies the product action of G_0 on X' is properly proximal. Since the action is proper, Lemma 1.3 gives proper proximality of G_0; proper proximality passes to finite-index overgroups by BIP21, Proposition 1.6, so G is properly proximal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.13 (almost every Weyl chamber is opposite a fixed chamber for the building harmonic measure)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a locally finite thick affine building with no tree factor in the setup of Section 2.3. Let Δ be the compact space of Weyl chambers in the spherical building at infinity of X. For a special vertex o in X, let μ_o be the standard probability measure on Δ characterized by μ_o(Ω_o(z)) = 1/N_{σ(o,z)}, where Ω_o(z) is the set of Weyl chambers whose sector based at o contains z, σ(o,z) is the vectorial distance, and N_{σ(o,z)} is the cardinality of the corresponding vectorial sphere. Then for every Weyl chamber C_0 in Δ, μ_o-almost every Weyl chamber C is opposite to C_0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a special vertex x and Weyl group element w, define Δ_x^w(C_0) as the set of chambers C such that the sectors Q(x,C) and Q(x,C_0) lie in a common apartment and have Weyl distance w. Also define Y_λ^w(x,C_0) as the set of vertices y at vectorial distance λ from x for which Ω_x(y) meets Δ_x^w(C_0). A vectorial horofunction computation shows Y_λ^w(x,C_0) = {y in V_λ(x) : h_{x,C_0}(y) = wλ}. Parkinson's counting results imply that the cardinality of this set, and therefore μ_x(Δ_x^w(C_0)), is independent of C_0. If w is not the longest Weyl group element w_0, choose a nonmaximal simplex u such that for distinct chambers C,C' in the infinite residue Res(u), the sets Δ_x^w(C) and Δ_x^w(C') are disjoint. Since all these sets have the same μ_x-measure and Res(u) is infinite by thickness, that common measure must be 0. The union over special vertices x of the non-opposite sets is countable, so μ_o gives measure 0 to chambers at Weyl distance w≠w_0 from C_0. Thus almost every chamber is opposite C_0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.16 (proper proximality for groups acting on affine buildings)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group admitting a proper minimal isometric action on a locally finite thick affine building X, and assume G has no finite orbit in the visual boundary ∂∞X. Then G is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First assume X has no tree factor. The compact spaces used are the finitely many spaces Δ_I of simplices of fixed type I in the spherical building at infinity. They carry projected measures μ_I from the harmonic measure μ_o on Weyl chambers. Lemma 2.18, using Adams-Ballmann's theorem and Lemma 2.10, shows none of the Δ_I carries a G-invariant probability measure: an invariant measure on Δ_I would give one on ∂∞X, forcing a fixed boundary point or a Euclidean factor, contrary to the hypotheses in this building setting. For a sequence g_n escaping compact sets, Papasoglu-Swenson's CAT(0) boundary theorem gives, after subsequence, points m,p in ∂∞X such that points outside a Tits ball near m have limit points near p. Choose a simplex type I and σ in Δ_I so that p lies in the interior position corresponding to σ. Let C_0 be a chamber containing m. By Proposition 2.13, μ_o-almost every chamber C is opposite C_0; such a chamber contains points at Tits distance π from m. Perturbing inside C and using the Papasoglu-Swenson convergence shows that the type-I face τ of C satisfies g_nτ → σ. Hence μ_I-almost every τ in Δ_I converges to σ, and Lemma 1.8 gives proper proximality of the action; properness gives proper proximality of G via Lemma 1.3. In the general case, decompose X = Y × Z where Z is a product of tree factors and Y has no tree factor. Minimality and the no-finite-boundary-orbit hypothesis pass to the factors. The above argument treats Y, while Z is a proper CAT(0) cube complex handled by Theorem 2.9 after passing to a finite-index subgroup. Lemma 1.4 combines the factor actions, Lemma 1.3 gives proper proximality of the finite-index subgroup, and BIP finite-index stability gives proper proximality of G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6 (compact metrizability of the HHS compactification)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,S) be a hierarchically hyperbolic space, assume X is proper and S is countable. Let ∂X be the Durham-Hagen-Sisto HHS boundary. Then X ∪ ∂X is compact and metrizable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The compactness is exactly Durham-Hagen-Sisto, Theorem 3.4. Metrizability is attributed to Hagen, Proposition 2.1. The lemma is used so that the HHS compactification can serve as a compact metrizable G-space in Lemma 1.6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Facts 3.3 and 3.5 (HHS boundary convergence criteria)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,S) be a hierarchically hyperbolic space with principal domain S_0 and principal hyperbolic space C S_0. A boundary point ξ in ∂X is represented as a finite sum ξ = Σ_S a_S^ξ ξ_S over a nonempty finite set Supp(ξ) of pairwise orthogonal domains S, with ξ_S in ∂∞C S, positive coefficients a_S^ξ, and total coefficient 1. Fact 3.3 says that a sequence x_n in X converges to ξ if and only if: for every S in Supp(ξ), π_S(x_n) converges to ξ_S in C S ∪ ∂∞C S; and for every S equal to an element of Supp(ξ) or orthogonal to every element of Supp(ξ), and every S' in Supp(ξ), the ratio d_{C S}(π_S(x_0),π_S(x_n)) / d_{C S'}(π_{S'}(x_0),π_{S'}(x_n)) tends to a_S^ξ/a_{S'}^ξ. Fact 3.5 gives the analogous criterion for a sequence ξ_n in ∂∞C S_0 converging to a point ξ in ∂X \ ∂∞C S_0: replace π_S(x_n) by the boundary projection ∂π_S(ξ_n), and require the same convergence and ratio conditions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These facts are unpacked directly from Durham-Hagen-Sisto's definition of the HHS boundary topology. In the paper they are used to prove convergence of images g_nξ of principal-boundary points by comparing their nonprincipal projections with projections of actual points g_n y in X.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7 (north-south dynamics on the HHS boundary from escaping sequences)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,S) be a hierarchically hyperbolic space with X proper. Let G be a countable group acting nonelementarily by HHS automorphisms on (X,S), where nonelementary means that the induced action on the principal hyperbolic space C S_0 contains two independent loxodromic isometries. Assume the G-action on X is by uniform quasi-isometries and has an almost equivariant principal projection, meaning there is C≥0 such that d_{C S_0}(π_{S_0}(gx), gπ_{S_0}(x))≤C for all g in G and x in X. If x is in X and (g_n) is a sequence in G escaping every compact subset of X, then there exist ξ_- and ξ_+ in ∂X and a subsequence (g_{σ(n)}) such that for every ξ in ∂∞C S_0 \ {ξ_-}, one has g_{σ(n)}ξ → ξ_+ in ∂X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since X is proper and the action is by uniform quasi-isometries, d(x,g_nx) and d(x,g_n^{-1}x) diverge. Compactness of X ∪ ∂X gives a subsequence, still denoted g_n, such that g_nx → ξ_+ and g_n^{-1}x → ξ_-; the same limits hold if x is replaced by any fixed y in X. Fix ξ in ∂∞C S_0 \ {ξ_-}. The first key claim is that for every p in C S_0, the sequence g_n^{-1}p does not converge to ξ in C S_0 ∪ ∂∞C S_0; otherwise almost equivariance of π_{S_0} and Fact 3.4 would force g_n^{-1}x → ξ in X ∪ ∂X, contradicting ξ≠ξ_-. Hyperbolicity of C S_0 then gives a point z on a quasigeodesic ray to ξ, uniformly far along the ray, such that the Gromov products ⟨g_n^{-1}p,ξ⟩_z are uniformly bounded. Applying g_n gives uniformly bounded ⟨p,g_nξ⟩_{g_nz}. If ξ_+ lies in ∂∞C S_0, then g_np → ξ_+ in the principal hyperbolic compactification, and the bounded Gromov product forces g_nξ → ξ_+ in ∂∞C S_0, hence in ∂X. If ξ_+ is not in ∂∞C S_0, write ξ_+ = Σ_S a_S ξ_S over pairwise orthogonal domains. Choose z, and then a point ~z in X with π_{S_0}(~z) close to z, sufficiently far so that geodesics from π_{S_0}(g_n~z) to g_nξ avoid uniform neighborhoods of the bounded subsets X_S associated to every S in Supp(ξ_+) or orthogonal to all of Supp(ξ_+). The bounded geodesic image axiom then makes ∂π_S(g_nξ) uniformly close to ρ_{S}^{S_0}(π_{S_0}(g_n~z)), and the consistency axiom makes this uniformly close to π_S(g_n~z). Since g_n~z → ξ_+, the HHS convergence criteria Facts 3.3 and 3.5 imply g_nξ → ξ_+.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1 (proper proximality of nonelementary HHS actions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,S) be a hierarchically hyperbolic space with X proper. Let G be a countable group acting nonelementarily by HHS automorphisms on (X,S), assume the G-action on X is by uniform quasi-isometries, and assume the action has an almost equivariant principal projection to the principal hyperbolic space C S_0. Then the G-action on X is properly proximal. If in addition the G-action on X is proper, then G is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Lemma 1.6 with K = X ∪ ∂X, which is compact metrizable by Lemma 3.6, and with K* = ∂∞C S_0 embedded in ∂X. Since the action on C S_0 is nonelementary, ∂∞C S_0 contains a Cantor set, for instance the limit set of a Schottky subgroup generated by independent loxodromics. The proof notes that nonelementarity also prevents K from carrying a G-invariant probability measure. Proposition 3.7 gives the required one-exception convergence for every sequence escaping compact subsets of X. Lemma 1.6 gives proper proximality of the action, and if the action on X is proper, Lemma 1.3 gives proper proximality of G.

proof_techniques.name: Sequential action criterion as a bridge to ultrafilters
proof_techniques.description: Instead of checking the BIP ultrafilter definition directly, the paper introduces a sequential notion of a properly proximal action on a proper metric space. One proves that every sequence escaping compact sets has a subsequence along which a boundary measure becomes asymptotically insensitive to right multiplication by any fixed group element. Lemma 1.3 then converts this sequential statement into ultrafilter proper proximality of the group, using compactness of probability-measure spaces and properness of the action.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a graph product von Neumann algebra, this suggests proving a sequential escape criterion for words or bimodule vectors first, and only afterward converting it into the formal proper proximality condition. Properness of a geometric action is replaced in the von Neumann setting by whatever notion forces sequences outside finite or relatively compact pieces.

proof_techniques.name: Diffuse measure on a boundary subset with one exceptional repelling point
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 1.6 isolates a reusable north-south principle. If a compact G-boundary K contains a G-invariant Borel subset K* with a Cantor subset, and every escaping sequence admits a subsequence that sends every point of K* except possibly one to a common attracting point, then any diffuse measure supported on K* witnesses proper proximality. The exceptional point is harmless because diffuse measures have no atoms, and Lemma 1.5 turns pointwise almost-everywhere convergence into weak-star convergence of pushed measures.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a graph product setting, one can look for a compact boundary of infinite reduced words, rays, or states where multiplication has convergence away from one forbidden prefix or direction. The trace-zero unitary hypothesis is a natural source of non-atomic or diffuse boundary data in an operator-algebraic analogue.

proof_techniques.name: Finite family of boundary pieces with quasi-invariant measures
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 1.8 allows proper proximality to be witnessed by finitely many compact spaces rather than a single boundary. The hypothesis is weaker than one-exception convergence: for each escaping sequence, it is enough that on one of the finitely many compact spaces, almost every point with respect to a quasi-invariant measure is attracted to a single limit. This is essential in the affine-building proof, where different simplex types at infinity are used as separate compact spaces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the graph product von Neumann algebra has several natural boundary strata indexed by cliques, syllable supports, or graph types, this finite-family criterion suggests not forcing all dynamics onto one compactification. Irreducibility may be used to rule out invariant measures on each stratum.

proof_techniques.name: Coordinate escape in products
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 1.4 proves that proper proximality of actions is stable under finite products when a sequence escaping the product must escape in at least one coordinate. The compact spaces for the product action are just the compact spaces from the factor actions, viewed through coordinate projections. Surjectivity of the subgroup projections ensures that absence of invariant measures is inherited from the factors.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph joins correspond to product or tensor decompositions in many graph-product contexts. The lemma gives a clean model for handling decomposable cases and clarifies why irreducibility, i.e. absence of a nontrivial join decomposition, is the case where one expects a single nonelementary direction.

proof_techniques.name: Rank-one CAT(0) boundary package: Morse Cantor set plus Papasoglu-Swenson convergence
proof_techniques.description: For rank-one CAT(0) actions, the paper constructs the boundary subset K* from Morse or visibility rays. A free subgroup generated by independent rank-one elements gives a quasi-isometrically embedded tree whose boundary is a Cantor set of uniformly Morse rays. Papasoglu-Swenson's theorem then supplies convergence of escaping sequences on all visibility points except possibly one. Together with absence of invariant measures on the visual boundary, this proves proper proximality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a geometric blueprint for graph-product groups acting on CAT(0) cube complexes, and it suggests an operator-algebraic analogue: identify rank-one-like reduced words whose powers have north-south behavior on a boundary and use a Cantor family of independent directions to build diffuse measures.

proof_techniques.name: CAT(0) cube rank-rigidity reduction
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 2.9 combines Caprace-Sageev rank rigidity with the rank-one CAT(0) criterion. A nonelementary proper cubical action is first restricted to an essential invariant subcomplex; after finite index, the complex splits as a product of irreducible factors; nonelementarity on each factor yields rank-one elements; proper proximality follows factorwise and then by the product lemma.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The irreducibility assumption in the original graph problem resembles the non-product hypothesis in cubical rank rigidity. This proof suggests reducing any graph-product argument to showing that the irreducible graph forces rank-one or nonelementary behavior, while join decompositions should be treated by product stability.

proof_techniques.name: No invariant boundary measure from independent north-south dynamics
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.6 shows a common way to verify the no invariant probability condition. A rank-one element forces every invariant measure to be supported on its two fixed points because its iterates have north-south dynamics. A second rank-one element with disjoint fixed points gives a contradiction. The same philosophy appears in the HHS proof, where nonelementary action on the principal hyperbolic space rules out invariant measures.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For graph product von Neumann algebras, one should look for two incompatible proximal directions generated by trace-zero unitaries in different noncommuting parts. Such directions can rule out invariant states or means on a proposed boundary object.

proof_techniques.name: Building opposition and harmonic measure
proof_techniques.description: In affine buildings, ordinary pointwise north-south dynamics on the full boundary is replaced by a finite collection of simplex-type compacta Δ_I. The harmonic measure μ_o on chambers has the key property that almost every chamber is opposite any fixed chamber. Papasoglu-Swenson convergence in the Tits boundary then implies that for a suitable type I, almost every simplex of that type is attracted to one limit simplex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique is useful if a graph-product boundary naturally has transversality or opposition rather than global one-exception convergence. A measure saying that a random boundary point is transverse to any fixed finite obstruction could replace the building harmonic measure.

proof_techniques.name: HHS principal hyperbolic projection controls boundary dynamics
proof_techniques.description: For hierarchically hyperbolic spaces, the paper proves north-south-type convergence on the HHS compactification by using the principal hyperbolic space C S_0. If an escaping sequence has forward and backward limits ξ_+ and ξ_-, then any principal-boundary point ξ≠ξ_- is sent to ξ_+. The proof uses hyperbolic Gromov products in C S_0, the bounded geodesic image axiom, consistency of projections, and the HHS boundary convergence criteria.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Many graph products of groups admit hierarchical structures, and reduced-word combinatorics often has a principal hyperbolic or contact-graph-like projection. Even for von Neumann graph products, this suggests proving convergence by first controlling a main coarse projection and then using consistency to control subordinate components.

proof_techniques.name: Diffuse Cantor sets from Schottky subgroups
proof_techniques.description: Both the CAT(0) and HHS arguments use nonelementarity to produce a Cantor set in the boundary: in CAT(0), via a quasi-isometrically embedded tree coming from independent rank-one elements; in HHS, via the limit set of a Schottky subgroup generated by independent loxodromics in the principal hyperbolic space. This Cantor set is used only to support a diffuse probability measure, not to carry the whole dynamics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The trace-zero unitary condition at each vertex may be used to produce nontrivial independent directions or free-product-like substructures. A Cantor family of boundary states or paths could provide the diffuse measure required for proper proximality.

other_useful_info: The introduction notes that BIP21, Theorem 4.3, makes the finite-family definition of proper proximality equivalent to a formulation with a single compact G-space, although finite families are convenient in proofs. The paper repeatedly uses BIP21, Proposition 1.6, for stability of proper proximality under passing to finite-index overgroups, and in the mapping-class-group section also uses the obstruction that groups with infinite FC-center are inner amenable and not properly proximal. The paper itself proves proper proximality for countable groups and discusses consequences for group von Neumann algebras and group-measure-space factors; it does not treat graph product von Neumann algebras directly. The CAT(0) cubical result, Theorem 2.9, is the closest group-theoretic analogue to graph-product situations where a graph product group acts on a Davis or quasi-median CAT(0)-cubical object. Theorem 4.1, quoted from BIP21, says that if G is properly proximal then LG has no weakly compact Cartan subalgebra, and for essentially free ergodic pmp actions G↷(X,μ), the crossed product L∞(X)⋊G has the expected uniqueness or nonexistence of weakly compact Cartan subalgebras depending on whether the action is weakly compact.
why_relevant: Provides geometric criteria for proper proximality for groups acting on CAT(0) spaces and CAT(0) cube complexes. It is useful for understanding the group-theoretic graph-product analogue and for importing boundary/proximality ideas from non-positive curvature.
